content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance ••• Administration ••• Executive/Legal ••• Enforcement ••• Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660            322-5901           322-6441          322-4444

December 27, 1978

Daniel V. Blackstock
County Counsel
Butte County
P.O. Box 1560
Oroville, CA 95965

Re: Opinion Request
     No. 78-014

Dear Mr. Blackstock:

     Thank you for your letter dated December 7, 1978, requesting an opinion regarding the disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act"). Since your question raises no substantial questions of interpretation under the Act, no formal opinion will be issued. However, I hope the following advice provided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) will be helpful.

     The facts as I understand them are as follows: the County of Butte wishes to contract for the services of an independent appraiser. The appraiser with whom the county wishes to contract is the father of Jane Dolan, a recently elected supervisor. It is our understanding that Ms. Dolan has been self-supporting for the past 11 years. She does not receive income from her father, nor has she been financially assisted by her father in any way during the past 11 years. The question you have asked us to consider is whether Butte County may contract with Ms. Dolan's father to perform real estate appraisals for the county.

     Implicit in your question is whether Supervisor Dolan may be disqualified from voting on the appraisal contract matter because it may financially benefit her father. Government Code Section 87100 provides:1/

     No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in

_________________________
1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise provided.

Daniel V. Blackstock
December 27, 1978
Page Two

any way attempt to use his official position to in-
fluence a governmental decision in which he knows or
has reason to know he has a financial interest.

The Act provides in Section 87103 that an official has
a financial interest in a governmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally, on a source of income, a direct or indirect
investment in a business entity worth more than $1,000, or
any direct or indirect interest in real property worth more
than $1,000. Section 87103 defines an indirect investment
or interest to mean any investment or interest owned by the
spouse or dependent child of a public official.

Supervisor Dolan must disqualify herself from partici-
pating in those matters in which she, her spouse or a dependent
child may have a financial interest. She is not precluded
from participating in decisions which may have a material
financial effect upon her father unless he provided income
to her of $250 or more in the year preceding the decision
in question. Since the facts indicate that Ms. Dolan has
not received income from her father for the past 11 years,2/
her father is not a source of income to her under the Act.
Therefore, it is our view that Ms. Dolan may participate in
those decisions involving the hiring of her father as a real
estate appraiser for Butte County.

In your letter you asked whether Butte County may contract
with Supervisor Dolan's father. The Political Reform Act
does not prohibit the county from contracting with parents
of public officials. However, Government Code Section 1090
may be relevant to this question now before our office for
consideration. Because the Commission has no jurisdiction
under Section 1090, it will not offer an opinion interpreting
this provision of the law as it relates to this matter. We
suggest that you contact the Attorney General's office with
respect to clarifying Section 1090's applicability to the
contract the county wishes to enter.

I hope that this letter has been helpful. If you have
any questions regarding this matter, please contact me or

2/ We do not know whether Ms. Dolan received any gifts
from her father. Gifts from certain relatives, including an
individual's parents, are specifically excluded from the
definition of gift under the Act. Section 82028.

Daniel V. Blackstock
December 27, 1978
Page Three


Dwight Dickerson, an attorney in our Conflicts of Interest
Division. If you wish to appeal the denial of your request
for a formal opinion, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18321.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:kp

cc: Jane Dolan
338 W. 18th Street
Chico, CA  95926

sections.question: Whether Butte County may contract with Ms. Dolan's father to perform real estate appraisals for the county, and whether Supervisor Dolan may be disqualified from voting on the appraisal contract matter because it may financially benefit her father.
sections.conclusion: Supervisor Dolan must disqualify herself from participating in those matters in which she, her spouse or a dependent child may have a financial interest. She is not precluded from participating in decisions which may have a material financial effect upon her father unless he provided income to her of $250 or more in the year preceding the decision in question. Since the facts indicate that Ms. Dolan has not received income from her father for the past 11 years, her father is not a source of income to her under the Act. Therefore, it is our view that Ms. Dolan may participate in those decisions involving the hiring of her father as a real estate appraiser for Butte County. The Political Reform Act does not prohibit the county from contracting with parents of public officials, though Government Code Section 1090 may be relevant.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a county may contract with the father of a newly elected supervisor for real estate appraisal services, and whether the supervisor must disqualify herself from voting on such a contract under the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Supervisor Dolan may participate in decisions regarding the hiring of her father because he is not a source of income to her under the Political Reform Act. However, the FPPC does not address Government Code Section 1090, which may impose additional restrictions on such contracting.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document states 'no formal opinion will be issued' and advice is provided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b), indicating this is informal advice rather than a formal opinion. Clearly structured with facts, analysis, and conclusion despite note that section headers were not found. Citation note indicates footnotes present in original.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Butte County may contract with Ms. Dolan's father to perform real estate appraisals for the county, and whether Supervisor Dolan may be disqualified from voting on the appraisal contract matter because it may financially benefit her father.

CONCLUSION: Supervisor Dolan must disqualify herself from participating in those matters in which she, her spouse or a dependent child may have a financial interest. She is not precluded from participating in decisions which may have a material financial effect upon her father unless he provided income to her of $250 or more in the year preceding the decision in question. Since the facts indicate that Ms. Dolan has not received income from her father for the past 11 years, her father is not a source of income to her under the Act. Therefore, it is our view that Ms. Dolan may participate in those decisions involving the hiring of her father as a real estate appraiser for Butte County. The Political Reform Act does not prohibit the county from contracting with parents of public officials, though Government Code Section 1090 may be relevant.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that a county may contract with the father of a supervisor and that the supervisor may participate in voting on such a contract, provided the parent has not been a source of income to the official in the preceding year. The FPPC declines to address potential restrictions under Government Code Section 1090, referring the county counsel to the Attorney General.
